q: In a Dataverse one-to-many (1:N) relationship, where does the lookup column live and how many parents can a child have?
o: On the child table, and a child associates with exactly one parent
On the parent table, and a child can have many parents
On both tables, with unlimited parents
On neither table; it is inferred at run time
why: 1:N places the lookup on the child (related) table; each child row points to a single parent row.

q: A developer says the relationship is 'N:1'. How does that relate to a 1:N relationship?
o: It is the same physical relationship viewed from the child pointing to the parent
It is a completely separate many-to-many relationship
It requires an intersect table to exist
It only works in model-driven apps
why: N:1 (many-to-one) is the same 1:N relationship seen from the child's angle.

q: You need a lookup that can point to EITHER an account or a contact row. Which Dataverse feature enables this?
o: A multi-table (polymorphic) lookup, e.g. a Customer data type
why: Multi-table (polymorphic) lookups, like the Customer type, let one column reference rows from several tables.

q: Decide whether each statement about Dataverse relationships is true.

rows.s: Dataverse supports one-to-many and many-to-many relationships.
A one-to-many relationship's child can be associated with only one parent.
Activity tables' regarding column can point to any activity-enabled table.

why: Dataverse offers 1:N and N:N; a 1:N child has one parent; activity regarding lookups are polymorphic.

q: Given a 1:N relationship, which is the SIMPLER way to list a selected location's desks instead of Filter()?
o: A nested ForAll over both tables
Adding the desk table as a second data source
why: Dot notation across a 1:N relationship (Selected.Desks) returns related rows concisely; you can still add Filter for extra criteria.

q: From a desk (child) row, how do you show its location's address using relationship navigation?
why: You can navigate the many-to-one from child to parent with dot notation: ThisItem.Location.Address.

q: Using Patch to set a lookup column on a desk, what value must you assign to the Location column?
o: A record from the primary (Location) table
The integer ID as a string
why: Patch sets a lookup by assigning a whole record from the primary table, e.g. {Location: FilterLocation_1.Selected}.

q: Which pair of functions associates and disassociates related rows without Patch?
why: Relate() links rows across a relationship; Unrelate() removes the association.

q: When establishing a MANY-TO-MANY relationship with Relate(), why doesn't the parameter order matter?
o: An N:N relationship has no primary table, so neither side is the parent
Relate always sorts its parameters alphabetically
N:N requires exactly one record on each side
why: N:N is symmetric with no primary table, so which record is first/second is irrelevant.

q: Adding an N:N field to a form throws errors on submit. Which workaround is described?
o: Clear the Update property and manually create the associations after submit
Convert the relationship to a choices column
why: For N:N on a form you clear Update and use Relate() logic (e.g. on the check icon) to associate the saved selections after submitting.

q: You must record HOW LONG a contact has spoken each language, not just that they speak it. What design handles this?
o: A custom intersect table with N:1 relationships to Contact and Language plus its own columns
A single choices column on Contact
why: A pure N:N can't store attributes about the association; a custom intersect table (with N:1 links) can hold extra data like duration/proficiency.

q: Decide whether each statement comparing choices columns and N:N relationships is true.

rows.s: Choices values are predetermined by the maker and can't be secured or disabled per row.
Rows in an N:N related table can be secured, deactivated and added at run time.
An N:N relationship by itself can store extra attributes about the association.

why: Choices are static/maker-defined; N:N related rows are runtime-flexible but the relationship alone stores no extra data.

q: Gallery1 shows customers; Gallery2 should show only the selected customer's invoices. What Items formula on Gallery2 works?
why: Filtering the child table by the selected parent's ID shows just that customer's invoices.

q: From an invoice (child) you know only CustomerID and want the customer's NAME. Which function retrieves that single parent record?
why: LookUp returns the single matching record from a data source, letting you read the parent's columns.

q: Showing a raw CustomerID to users adds little value. What is the better display approach for the gallery?
o: Use LookUp on the parent table to display the customer name instead of the ID
Convert the ID column to a choice
Store the ID in a global variable
why: Replace the ID with a friendly value by looking up the related parent record's name.

q: Decide whether each statement about parent/child navigation in Power Apps is true.

rows.s: The parent Customer table needs no direct reference to the child Invoice table for the filter to work.
Text(ThisItem.InvoiceDate, DateTimeFormat.ShortDate) formats an invoice date for display.

why: The child holds the foreign key, so the parent needs no reference; Text() with a DateTimeFormat renders dates.

q: In the exercise, which function adds the selected gallery item into the cart collection colCart?
why: Collect appends a record (ThisItem) to the collection.

q: At check-out the code loops every cart item to create a line item per product. Which function performs that loop?
why: ForAll iterates the colCart table, running Collect for each record to build ReceiptLineItems.

q: The pattern If(IsEmpty(ReceiptHeader), 1, Last(ReceiptHeader).ID + 1) is used to do what?
o: Generate an auto-incrementing ID for the new record
why: It creates a simple autonumber: 1 when empty, otherwise the last ID plus one.

q: Decide whether each statement about the relational cart exercise is true.

rows.s: LookUp(Products, ID=ThisItem.ProductID).Name resolves a line item's product name.
Filter(ReceiptLineItems, ReceiptHeaderID=Gallery3.Selected.ID) links a receipt's lines to the selected header.
Clear(colCart) is unnecessary after submitting an order.

why: LookUp resolves names, Filter ties line items to their header, and Clear(colCart) empties the cart after check-out.
